title: NASA | Solid-like Materials
description: Why do Brazil nuts end up on top of the mix? Why do footprints on wet sand seem to dry the ground? How can a pile of snow turn into an avalanche—and what does that have to do with exploring the Moon? This learning pathway dives into the curious, surprising, and essential physics of granular materials—substances like sand, nuts, soil, and snow that behave like both solids and fluids. Through hands-on investigations, real-world analogies, and even NASA rover testing, you will explore the physical principles that govern granular behavior in everyday life and in planetary exploration.
learning_objectives: By the end of this learning pathway, you will be able to describe the physical properties of granular materials and explain their dual fluid-solid behavior under different forces.
Conduct investigations and interpret real-world phenomena like the Brazil nut effect, non-Newtonian flow, and the angle of repose.
Apply concepts from granular physics to real-world challenges in engineering and space exploration, including NASA’s rover development.

title: Further Applications of Newton's Laws: Friction, Drag, and Elasticity
description: This pathway further explores applications of Newton's laws of motion. The pathway discusses examples of specific contact forces, such as friction, air or liquid drag, and elasticity that may affect the motion or shape. It also discusses the nature of forces on both macroscopic and microscopic levels of an object. Newton's laws are applied to describe the motion of an object and to examine relationships between contact forces and other forces exerted on an object. The examples in this pathway provide practice in using vector properties of forces and free-body diagrams to determine net force.
learning_objectives: Discuss the general characteristics of friction.
Describe the various types of friction.
Calculate the magnitudes of static and kinetic frictional forces.
Define drag force and model it mathematically.
Discuss the applications of drag force.
Define terminal velocity.
Calculate and find terminal velocity.
Identify and state hooke's law.
Explain hooke's law using graphical representation between deformation and applied force.
Discuss the three types of deformations such as changes in length, sideways shear, and changes in volume.
Describe with examples the young's modulus, shear modulus, and bulk modulus.
Calculate the change in length given mass, length, and radius.

title: Applied Chemistry – Solutions, Materials, and Industry
description: Chemistry is everywhere, from solution preparation in labs to creating new materials that shape industries. This pathway explores how solutions are made, the properties of materials, and chemistry’s role in real-world industries like medicine and manufacturing. You will gain practical chemistry skills through interactive activities and career insights.
subject_tags: Chemistry:Molecular and Ionic Compound Structure and Properties
learning_objectives: By the end of this pathway, you will be able to explain how solutions are prepared and measured in laboratory settings.
Perform calculations related to molar mass and solution concentrations.
Analyze material properties and how atomic structures affect functionality.
Evaluate chemistry’s impact on industry, from medicine to engineering.
Explore career paths in chemistry and how professionals use these concepts daily.
